This off‑by‑one discrepancy should be corrected.

The lookback period for colonoscopy is set to **1 month**, which is clinically inappropriate for colon‑cancer screening. Current guidelines require checking for a colonoscopy performed within the **last 10 years** (≈120 months) when the result was normal. Additionally, the age threshold of **≥ 50 years** reflects older recommendations; the most recent USPSTF guidance lowers the starting age to **45 years**. Both values should be updated to align with current clinical practice guidelines.

The lookback period of **1 month** for assessing prior prostate cancer screening is clinically unreasonable; standard guidelines recommend a longer interval (typically 12 months or 1–2 years) for PSA testing and related assessments. The age threshold (≥ 50 years) and the specified value set OIDs for PSA test, digital rectal examination, and prostate biopsy are appropriate.

The age threshold is set to **≥ 25 years**, but major hypertension screening guidelines (e.g., USPSTF, ACC/AHA) recommend initiating routine blood‑pressure screening in adults **≥ 18 years** (or ≥ 20 years in some recommendations). This makes the age criterion more restrictive than the clinical practice guideline. The 6‑month look‑back interval is more frequent than the typical 1‑ to 2‑year interval recommended for average‑risk adults, though it may be acceptable for high‑risk groups. The value‑set identifier appears appropriate.

The entry labeled “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” contains the value **190 mg/dL**, which is a laboratory threshold for LDL cholesterol, not a time interval. A look‑back period should be expressed in units of time (e.g., “most recent LDL result within the past 12 months”). The age thresholds (≥30 years and ≤80 years) and the LDL cholesterol value set identifier are appropriate, but the mis‑classification of the LDL threshold as a time interval makes the implementation inconsistent with clinical practice guidelines.

The “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” entry lists **“9 g%”**, which is a laboratory result value (HbA1c percentage) rather than a temporal interval. Look‑back periods should be expressed in units of time (e.g., days, months, years). This mismatch indicates the ELM implementation incorrectly uses a clinical measurement as a time interval. All other entries (Age ≥ 18 years and the HbA1c value set OID) are appropriate.

The lower age threshold is set to ≥ 30 years, but cervical cancer screening guidelines recommend initiating Pap testing at age 21 (Pap alone every 3 years for ages 21‑29). Therefore the age lower bound is too high for a Pap‑test–only rule. The upper bound (≤ 65 years) and the 3‑year look‑back interval for Pap testing are appropriate.

The age threshold in the ELM is set to **Age ≥ 25 years**, which is higher than the guideline recommendation for alcohol misuse screening (typically **Age ≥ 18 years** for adults, with some guidelines starting at age 12). This discrepancy means eligible younger adults would be missed by the CDS. The look‑back period (1 year) and the value set OID (2.16.840.1.113883.3.600.1542) are appropriate.

The look‑back interval for “Has Recent DEXA Scan” is set to 6 months. Clinical guidelines recommend repeat bone‑density testing roughly every 2 years (24 months) for patients without new risk factors. A 6‑month window is too short and could incorrectly exclude patients who had a valid DEXA within the guideline‑recommended interval. All other values (age ≥ 65 years, female sex OID, and the listed value‑set OIDs) are clinically appropriate.

The age criteria are inconsistent with the clinical guideline for abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) screening. The USPSTF recommends a one‑time abdominal ultrasound for **men aged 65 – 75 years** who have ever smoked. The ELM implementation uses an age lower bound of 55 years (and does not restrict by gender), which is clinically inappropriate for this recommendation.
